briefing:
### The window in one sentence

The week of June 27 -- July 3 was dominated by a single-day traffic spike on June 29 that obscures what is otherwise a below-median traffic pattern, with sessions averaging 15 per day against a 28-day median closer to 20. GSC data is entirely absent this window, which is a data infrastructure problem that needs to be resolved before any meaningful search trend analysis is possible.

---

### Volume + composition

Over the seven days ending July 3, the site logged 105 sessions, 97 users, and 579 pageviews, with a daily session average of 15.0 and a daily user average of 13.9. Those numbers look passable in aggregate, but they are heavily skewed by June 29, which alone contributed 30 sessions and 339 pageviews -- more than half of total pageviews for the entire week. Strip that day out and the remaining six days average just 12.5 sessions and 40 pageviews per day, which is meaningfully below the 28-day medians flagged by the anomaly system throughout the window. GSC impressions and clicks are reported as zero across every single day, which is not plausible for a live site -- this almost certainly reflects a broken GSC connection rather than actual search invisibility.

Channel composition this week ran 49% Direct (51 sessions), 43% Organic Search (45 sessions), 5% Organic Social (5 sessions), and 4% Referral (4 sessions). That Organic Search share is notably higher than BCC's baseline of roughly 25%, which in a normal week would be encouraging. Without GSC data to confirm what queries or pages drove it, though, the signal is incomplete. Direct traffic came in below its typical 60% baseline share, which may simply reflect the low-volume weekend days on either end of the window pulling that number down. The Referral number (4 sessions) is thin -- the site has 12 inbound links across 4 referring domains, so there is more referral potential on the table than is currently being activated.

---

### Trend (first half vs second half)

The halves comparison shows sessions at 43 in the first half (June 27-29) and 62 in the second half (June 30 -- July 3), a 44% increase that the system flags as positive momentum. That reading deserves significant skepticism. The first half includes June 28, the worst traffic day of the week (4 sessions, 75% below median), and June 29, the best day of the week (30 sessions, 50% above median). Those two days cancel each other out in a way that makes the first half look artificially depressed. Meanwhile, July 3 -- the final day of the second half -- logged only 8 sessions and an average session duration of 21 seconds, which is 86.5% below the 28-day median and suggests nearly all of that traffic was bouncing immediately, possibly from a bot pass or a misdirected link rather than genuine engagement. The honest read here is that the window is volatile and flat, not accelerating.

---

### Launches + efficacy

Everything logged as a "launch" in this window is a git commit -- daily pulse, SEO pulse, and marketing pulse entries -- none of which correspond to published public pages with URLs, SERP positions, or measurable GSC or GA4 attribution. The launches ledger recorded 20 commits across the seven-day window, all low-confidence, auto-detected entries. None carry impressions, clicks, or session counts. This is consistent activity for maintaining the pulse reporting infrastructure, but it represents zero net new content surface area for organic search to index and rank.

The 30-day efficacy rollup is the more meaningful number here. Across the last 30 days, 12 pages and 61 page modifications have accumulated 96,278 GSC impressions and 268 clicks attributed, with 335 GA4 sessions, 1 ranking in the top 3, and 2 rankings in the top 10. That is a real content footprint producing real impressions, but a 0.28% click-through rate on 96K impressions is low. The top-3 ranking is presumably pulling a meaningful share of those 268 clicks, and the two top-10 rankings likely account for most of the rest. What that tells us is that the pages outside those top positions are generating impressions without generating clicks -- a CTR and snippet quality problem more than a content volume problem. There are no new backlinks logged for this specific window beyond the existing 12 total (4 referring domains, 5 of which are .edu links, which is a genuine asset).

---

### What this means + what to do next

1. **Fix the GSC connection before anything else.** Seven consecutive days of zero impressions is not a traffic problem -- it is a data problem. Without GSC, there is no way to know which pages are ranking, which queries are moving, or whether the 30-day rollup numbers are current. Everything else on this list is lower priority than restoring that feed.

2. **Investigate June 29 and July 2 to understand what drove the spikes.** June 29 produced 30 sessions and 339 pageviews; July 2 produced 21 sessions and 98 pageviews, both significantly above median. Neither day has a corresponding content launch. Check GA4 source/medium detail for those two days -- if a specific referrer, social post, or direct URL is responsible, that is a repeatable traffic source worth deliberately activating again.

3. **Prioritize CTR improvement on pages with high impressions, low clicks.** The 30-day rollup shows 96K impressions producing only 268 clicks. Next week's content work should focus on auditing title tags and meta descriptions for the pages generating the most impressions but ranking outside the top 3, not on producing more new pages. Tightening snippet copy on even a handful of those pages could move click volume more than new content at this stage.

4. **Develop a plan to activate the existing backlink profile.** Four referring domains and 12 inbound links -- five of them from .edu sources -- is a stronger foundation than many comparable practices have, but 4 referral sessions in seven days suggests those links are either buried, non-crawlable, or pointing to pages that do not hold visitors. Identify which .edu domains are linking, confirm the linked pages are live and well-formed, and consider whether a follow-up outreach to one of those institutions could produce a second, higher-prominence placement.

5. **Establish a baseline for what a normal week looks like.** The anomaly system flagged 21 CRITICAL events in seven days, which is high enough to suggest the median baselines may need recalibration, or that the site genuinely lacks a stable weekly traffic pattern. Before drawing conclusions from future windows, it is worth pulling a 60-day session trend to determine whether 15 sessions/day is actually low or whether the 28-day medians are being inflated by past spikes.
